---
#title: "Monty + Sue — Two Sisters"
#subtitle: "Trad. — with arrangement and annotation notes"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: songs.css
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Contents"
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    geometry:
      - top=2cm
      - bottom=2cm
      - left=1.8cm
      - right=1.8cm
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \pagestyle{empty}
---

## The Two Sisters (Oh the Wind & Rain) {.song}

*TRAD. — Peggy Seeger / Bay Whitaker / Fates*

::: {.structure-label}
Intro – our version
:::

__*There are many versions of this Scottish Ballad, this Scottish tale, variously known as `The Two Sisters`, `The Twa Sisters`, `Binnorie` and more. This is our version. We hope you like it.*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
[A]{.chord} [B]{.chord} [D]{.chord}\
Twas early one morning in the month of May

[Em]{.chord} [A]{.chord}\
*Oh the wind and rain;*

[A]{.chord} [D]{.chord}\
Two sisters went fishing on a hot summer's day,

[Em]{.chord} [D]{.chord} [A]{.chord}\
*[CRYING]{.stage} Oh, the dreadful wind and rain.*
:::
:::

::: {.annotation}
[A]{.circled} — section repeat label
:::


__*As they were sat on the river bank, singing songs to each other, they saw their brother approaching. And there was someone with him, another young man. And, oh, but he was handsome...*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
[A]{.chord} [D]{.chord} [Em – A]{.chord}\
Two sweet sisters side by side / *Oh…*

[A]{.chord} [D]{.chord} [Em – D – A]{.chord}\
Both of them want to be a good man's bride / *Oh… [CRYING]{.stage}*
:::
:::

__*At first he courted the elder sister, and it seemed as if they would become betrothed to each other. But then the young man's eyes started to wander - and on the day she thought he would ask for her hand, he told her she was not for him.*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
Johnny gave the young one a fine gold ring / *Oh… /*

Didn't give the other one anything / *Oh…*
:::
:::

__*The elder sister, jilted, spurned, was furious. She swore to herself she would have her revenge. And suggested to to the younger, that they take a walk down by the riverside, sing the song they used to sing in the time before.*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
Two sweet sisters walking by the stream / *Oh… /*

One came behind & pushed the other one in / *Oh…*
:::
:::

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
She pushed her in to the river to drown / *Oh… /*

And watched her as she floated down / *Oh…*
:::
:::

__*A scream as the younger sister was pushed into the river, held out her hand, pleading for help. "I won't take the hand that took my betrothed." "You can have him; and my fortune, my share of our inheritance." "I will have it anyway," and the elder sister and walked away.*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
She floated on down to the miller's pond. / *Oh… /*

"Father, father, there comes a swan!" / *Oh…*
:::
:::

__*But it wasn't a swan...*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
The miller ran for his drifting hook / *Oh… /*

And brought that poor girl from the brook / *Oh…*
:::
:::

__*They did what they could but there was nothing they could do. And rather than bury her in finery, they took her dress and they took her jewels, and from that day on, though they didn't have much, the Miller's daughter did wear a pretty bracelet round her wrist, and from time to time, a pretty frock. And as for the body, they buried it in a shallow grave underneath the hedgerow along the side of the mill race.*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
A fiddler man came he came walking by / *Oh…*

He saw that poor girl lying there / *Oh…*
:::
:::

__*It was a fox, perhaps, that had scraped away at the shallow grave. And when the fiddler recognised the bones for what they were, he was reminded of an old tale, and old story, of the Devil's violin, at tale I would tell you if I had but the time.*__

__*And the fiddler thought that maybe he could do something with those bones.*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
He made a fiddle bow of her long yellow hair / *Oh… /*

He made fiddle pegs of her little finger bones / *Oh…*
:::
:::

__*Are you listening? This story's not necessarily for children. It gets worse...*__

::: {.verse}
::: {.bracket-group}
He made a fiddle of her little breast-bone / *Oh… /*

With a sound that could melt a heart of stone / *Oh…*

:::
:::

__*He placed the new fiddle carefully in his bag, then carried on his way, to a gig he had that night, a wedding reception for the daughter of a local lord, a happy event that was also tinged with sadness, as the anniversary of the day the bride's sister had left their home and gone who knows where.*__

__*And when he to got to the gig, he took his trusty old fiddle of out of his bag and he started to play.*__

*And he played as they ate, and he played as they danced, but wouldn't you know it, as he came to the end, of one of the tunes, one of the strings, broke.*__

__*So he took out the new fiddle, the one he'd crafted earlier that day; but before he had a chance to start to play, it began to play itself.*__


::: {.verse}
And the only tune that the fiddle would play / *Oh…*

The only tune that the fiddle would play, was / *Oh….*
:::

__*The bride's face paled, as she recognised the tune; and she whispered the words of the song she used to sing with her sister.*__

::: {.verse}
*Oh, the wind and rain.*

*The only tune that the fiddle would play, was oh the dreadful wind and rain*
:::
